one.zh: 传票异议:独立子女律师向健康机构发出传票调取父母及子女病历;母亲以缺乏正当取证目的、属「受保护私密信息」（s 102BA）为由提出八点异议。法院依 s 102BD(5) 亲自查阅文件后，认定其相关、不触发保护，驳回异议，仅准独立子女律师亲自查阅。
one.en: Subpoena objection: the Independent Children's Lawyer subpoenaed the parents' and children's health records; the mother objected on eight grounds amounting to lack of legitimate forensic purpose and protected confidences (s 102BA). After inspecting the documents itself under s 102BD(5), the court found them relevant, not protected, dismissed the objection, and allowed inspection by the ICL only.

issue.zh: 独立子女律师向健康机构调取父母与子女病历的传票，是否因缺乏正当取证目的、或落入「受保护私密信息」而应被异议成功阻止?
issue.en: Should a subpoena by the Independent Children's Lawyer for the parents' and children's health records be defeated by objection on grounds of lack of legitimate forensic purpose or that the material is a 'protected confidence'?

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Subpoena Objection – Where the Independent Children's Lawyer filed a subpoena to the O Health District for updating material in relation to the parents and childrens health records – Where the Respondent filed a Notice of Objection to the subpoena raising eight (8) grounds of objection which broadly amount to an assertion of a lack of legitimate forensic purpose and the material falls within a protected confidences pursuant to s 102BA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) – Where the Court heard oral submissions from the parties – Where the Court inspected the material pursuant to s 102BD(5) of the Act – Where the Court found that the material produced was relevant and did not warrant any direction being made pursuant to s102BD of the Act.

subseq.zh: 异议被驳回，仅准独立子女律师亲自查阅（不得复印）。法院保留在情况变化时（如父母日后聘请律师）放宽查阅限制的可能。实体程序仍部分待审，相关上诉已聆讯但尚未裁决。
subseq.en: The objection was dismissed; only the ICL may inspect, in person (no photocopying). The court left open relaxing that restriction if circumstances change (e.g. if the parents obtain representation). The substantive proceedings remain part-heard, with an appeal heard but not yet determined.

hint.en: The whole case as a story

html.zh:
这是一宗就传票异议所作的简短口头理由。独立子女律师（ICL）于 2026 年 3 月向 O Health District 发出传票，调取自 2025 年 3 月 18 日起，关于父母及两名子女的临床/医疗记录、评估、出入院、诊断、转介、来往函件等全部文件。母亲（自我代理）于 2026 年 4 月 9 日提出异议，列八点理由，归为两类:其一，依《1975 年家庭法》第 102BA 条主张属受保护私密信息;其二，缺乏正当取证目的。

背景:实体程序部分待审，涉两名子女，案情复杂。Altobelli 法官曾于 2025 年 11 月 19 日就子女的终局与临时命令发表理由（Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834），相关上诉已聆讯但尚未裁决;命令要求母亲、父亲与子女做若干事，包括参与治疗，案件将待审至本年稍晚。

处理方式:就取证目的，法律见 Waind and Hill 与 Hatton 两案。父母与 ICL 一致同意，由法院先自行查阅文件以裁定异议;法院依第 102BD(5) 条照办，且只查阅落入异议范围（即母亲、且在传票时间范围内）的文件。查阅结果显示，这些是母亲就其健康、并因本诉讼而与医疗/专职医护接触的例行记录。法院未能识别出落入或可能落入第 102BE 条的文件;考虑到法院长期审理本案及大量认定，也无法认定第 102BE(2) 条所定义的「损害」很可能或可能因此直接或间接对母亲或子女造成;即便如此，该证据的证明价值也足以使异议被驳回。

裁断:当事人被命令做若干事，传票的清晰且正当目的正是调取与此直接相关的信息;就「缺乏取证目的」（亦含传票范围之广）的异议无据——文件仅及 2025 年 3 月 18 日起，与剩余争点相称。异议驳回，仅准 ICL 暂时查阅。尽管认定不存在受保护私密信息，但鉴于父母目前均自我代理，仅准 ICL 查阅仍属适当、符合子女最佳利益;ICL 亦已表明不会复印、仅亲自查阅，法院予以采纳，但保留日后放宽（如父母聘请律师时）的余地。
html.en:
These are brief oral reasons on a subpoena objection. The Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) issued a subpoena in March 2026 to the O Health District for all clinical/medical records, assessments, admissions, diagnoses, referrals and correspondence from 18 March 2025 onwards relating to the parents and two children. The mother (self-represented) objected on 9 April 2026 on eight grounds, falling into two categories: a protected confidence under s 102BA of the Family Law Act 1975, and a lack of legitimate forensic purpose.

Background: the substantive proceedings are part-heard, complex, and concern two children. Altobelli J had given reasons on the children's final and interim orders on 19 November 2025 (Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834); an appeal has been heard but not yet determined. The orders require the mother, father and children to do certain things, including participating in therapy, and the case remains part-heard until later in the year.

Approach: on forensic purpose, the law is found in Waind and Hill and Hatton. The parents and the ICL agreed the court should first inspect the documents itself to rule on the objection; the court did so under s 102BD(5), reading only documents within the objection's parameters (the mother's, within the subpoena's timeframe). They proved to be routine records of the mother's engagement with medical and allied-health professionals about her health and arising from the litigation. The court could identify no document falling within s 102BE and, given its long involvement and extensive findings, could not conclude that 'harm' as defined in s 102BE(2) was likely or might be caused, directly or indirectly, to the mother or children; even if it were, the probative value of the evidence would predicate dismissal of the objection.

Decision: the parties were ordered to do certain things, and the subpoena's clear and legitimate purpose was to produce relevant information about exactly that; the objection on lack of forensic purpose (which here encapsulated the subpoena's width) had no foundation — the documents sought information only from 18 March 2025 and were proportionate to the remaining issues. The objection was dismissed and leave to inspect granted to the ICL only, for the time being. Despite finding no protected confidence, with both parents currently self-represented it remained appropriate and in the children's best interests that only the ICL inspect; the ICL had foreshadowed no photocopying and in-person inspection only, which the court accepted, while remaining open to relaxing the restriction (for example if the parents obtain representation).

html.zh:
性质:实体程序进行中的程序性裁定（传票异议），简短口头理由。不是终局判决，而是部分待审的复杂子女案件中的中间程序步骤。

脉络:ICL 于 2026 年 3 月发出传票 → 母亲 2026 年 4 月 9 日提出八点异议 → 2026 年 4 月 17 日聆讯（Newcastle，视频方式），听取双方口头陈词，法院依第 102BD(5) 条亲自查阅文件 → 2026 年 4 月 21 日作出裁定。背景判决见 Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834（2025-11-19），相关上诉已聆讯未决。
html.en:
Nature: a procedural ruling (a subpoena objection) made during ongoing substantive proceedings, with brief oral reasons. It is not a final judgment but an interlocutory step in a complex, part-heard children's case.

Flow: the ICL issued the subpoena in March 2026 → the mother objected on eight grounds on 9 April 2026 → hearing on 17 April 2026 (Newcastle, by videoconference), with oral submissions and the court inspecting the documents itself under s 102BD(5) → ruling on 21 April 2026. The background judgment is Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834 (19 Nov 2025), with an appeal heard but undetermined.

html.zh:
§ s102BA ›
§ s102BD ›
§ s102BE ›
§ s102BF ›
框架
传票异议涉《1975 年家庭法》Part XI 第 1B 分部（第 102BA–102BF 条）关于受保护私密信息的规定:第 102BA 条界定何为受保护私密信息;第 102BD 条规定法院处理此类异议的程序（第 (5) 款允许法院先自行查阅文件）;第 102BE 条规定法院须考量披露是否可能造成「损害」（第 (2) 款界定损害），并权衡证据的证明价值;第 102BF 条为相关配套条款。本案另以普通法上「正当取证目的」原则审查传票（见 Waind and Hill、Hatton 两案）。
html.en:
§ s102BA ›
§ s102BD ›
§ s102BE ›
§ s102BF ›
Framework
A subpoena objection engages Division 1B of Part XI of the Family Law Act 1975 (ss 102BA–102BF) on protected confidences: s 102BA defines a protected confidence; s 102BD sets out the procedure for the court to deal with such objections (sub (5) lets the court inspect the documents itself); s 102BE requires the court to weigh whether disclosure might cause 'harm' (defined in sub (2)) against the probative value of the evidence; and s 102BF is a related provision. The court also tested the subpoena against the common-law principle of 'legitimate forensic purpose' (see Waind and Hill and Hatton).

html.zh:
法院就传票的取证目的援引下列判例:

National Employers' Mutual v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372
确立传票须有正当取证目的、所调取文件须与争点相关的原则。
Hatton v Attorney-General (Cth) (2000) FLC 93-038
在家事语境下适用「正当取证目的」原则，审查传票的适当性。
Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834
同一双方的实体判决（2025-11-19），交代本案背景、命令及法院的大量认定。
html.en:
On the subpoena's forensic purpose the court relied on:

National Employers' Mutual v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372
Establishes that a subpoena must have a legitimate forensic purpose and seek documents relevant to the issues.
Hatton v Attorney-General (Cth) (2000) FLC 93-038
Applies the 'legitimate forensic purpose' principle in the family law context to test a subpoena's propriety.
Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834
The substantive judgment between the same parties (19 Nov 2025), giving the background, orders and the court's extensive findings.

html.zh:
1. 两类异议 —— 母亲八点异议归为:受保护私密信息（依第 102BA 条及第 1B 分部）与缺乏正当取证目的。

2. 由法院先行查阅 —— 经父母与 ICL 一致同意，法院依第 102BD(5) 条自行查阅文件，且只读落入异议范围（母亲、且在时间范围内）的文件。

3. 不构成受保护私密信息 —— 查阅显示这些是母亲就健康及因本诉讼而与医疗/专职医护接触的例行记录;法院未识别出落入第 102BE 条的文件，且鉴于其长期审理与大量认定，无法认定第 102BE(2) 条的「损害」很可能或可能直接/间接造成;即便如此，证据的证明价值也足以驳回异议。

4. 取证目的成立、范围相称 —— 当事人被命令做若干事，传票的清晰正当目的正是调取与此相关的信息;文件仅及 2025 年 3 月 18 日起，与剩余争点相称;故「缺乏取证目的」异议无据。

5. 处分与查阅限制 —— 异议驳回，仅准 ICL 暂时查阅。尽管不存在受保护私密信息，但因父母目前均自我代理，仅准 ICL 查阅仍属适当、符合子女最佳利益;ICL 已承诺仅亲自查阅、不复印，法院采纳，并保留日后（如父母聘请律师）放宽的余地。
html.en:
1. Two categories of objection — the mother's eight grounds reduced to: a protected confidence (under s 102BA and Division 1B) and a lack of legitimate forensic purpose.

2. The court inspects first — by agreement of the parents and the ICL, the court inspected the documents itself under s 102BD(5), reading only those within the objection's parameters (the mother's, within the timeframe).

3. No protected confidence — inspection showed routine records of the mother's engagement with medical and allied-health professionals about her health and arising from the litigation; the court identified no document within s 102BE and, given its long involvement and extensive findings, could not conclude that 'harm' under s 102BE(2) was likely or might be caused directly or indirectly; even so, the probative value of the evidence would predicate dismissal.

4. Forensic purpose made out, width proportionate — the parties were ordered to do certain things and the subpoena's clear, legitimate purpose was to obtain relevant information about that; the documents reached back only to 18 March 2025 and were proportionate to the remaining issues, so the lack-of-purpose objection had no foundation.

5. Disposition and inspection limits — the objection was dismissed and inspection granted to the ICL only, for the time being. Despite there being no protected confidence, with both parents self-represented it remained appropriate and in the children's best interests that only the ICL inspect; the ICL undertook in-person inspection only and no photocopying, which the court accepted, leaving open a later relaxation (for example, if the parents obtain representation).

html.zh:
裁判要旨

面对以「受保护私密信息」（第 102BA–102BF 条）与「缺乏正当取证目的」为由的传票异议，法院可依第 102BD(5) 条先自行查阅文件再作裁定;若文件相关、不触发第 102BE 条的损害、且证明价值足够，异议应予驳回。

即便认定不存在受保护私密信息，法院仍可基于子女最佳利益与程序公正，对查阅施加限制（如仅准独立子女律师亲自查阅、不得复印），并保留在情况变化时放宽的裁量。
html.en:
Ratio

Faced with a subpoena objection on grounds of 'protected confidence' (ss 102BA–102BF) and 'lack of legitimate forensic purpose', the court may inspect the documents itself first under s 102BD(5) before ruling; if the material is relevant, does not trigger s 102BE harm, and has sufficient probative value, the objection should be dismissed.

Even where it finds no protected confidence, the court may, in the children's best interests and for procedural fairness, place limits on inspection (such as inspection by the Independent Children's Lawyer only, in person, with no photocopying), retaining a discretion to relax them as circumstances change.

html.zh:
Subpoena · 传票
命令第三方向法院提交文件或出庭作证的法律文书。
Notice of Objection · 异议通知
当事人对传票提出反对的正式文书，列明反对理由。
Legitimate forensic purpose · 正当取证目的
传票所调取文件须与诉讼争点真实相关、有助于裁判，而非「钓鱼」。
Protected confidence · 受保护私密信息
依《家庭法》第 1B 分部受特别保护、披露可能造成损害的私密信息（如医疗记录）。
Probative value · 证明价值
证据对待证事实的证明力;法院据此与潜在损害相权衡。
Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL) · 独立子女律师
代表子女最佳利益、独立于父母的律师。
Part-heard · 部分待审
实体程序已开始但尚未审结，仍在进行中的状态。
html.en:
Subpoena
A legal document compelling a third party to produce documents to the court or to give evidence.
Notice of Objection
A formal document by which a party objects to a subpoena, setting out the grounds.
Legitimate forensic purpose
The requirement that documents sought by subpoena be genuinely relevant to the issues and assist adjudication, not a fishing expedition.
Protected confidence
Confidential information (such as medical records) given special protection under Division 1B of the Family Law Act, where disclosure might cause harm.
Probative value
The strength of evidence in proving a fact in issue; weighed by the court against potential harm.
Independent Children's Lawyer (ICL)
A lawyer representing the child's best interests, independent of the parents.
Part-heard
A state where the substantive hearing has begun but is not yet completed.

html.zh:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These brief oral reasons explain why a Notice of Objection – Subpoena (“notice of objection”) has been dismissed. The subpoena was filed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer on 27 March 2026 and is directed to the Proper Officer, O Health District. The documents sought are as follows:

A copy of all documents, including but not limited to, clinical notes, medical notes, attendance records, assessments, admissions, treatment diagnosis/prognosis, discharge summaries, medical certificates, referrals, correspondence and all other writings from 18 March 2025 to date in relation to the parents and two children.

BACKGROUND

The notice of objection was filed on 9 April 2026 by the Respondent Mother (“the mother”). There are eight stated grounds. There is no need to repeat them here. They fall into two broad categories:

(1) an objection based on protected confidences under s 102BA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”); and

(2) an objection based on lack of legitimate forensic purpose.

The substantive proceedings are part-heard. They are complex proceedings relating to two children. On 19 November 2025, I published reasons for judgment explaining both the final and the interim orders that I made in relation to the children (see Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834). An appeal has been lodged in relation to the orders, which has been heard, but not yet determined. For present purposes, the orders relevantly provide for both the mother, the father and the children to do certain things, including participating in therapy. The case is part-heard until later this year. An objection based on a protected confidence is based on Division 1B of Part XI of the Act, being s 102BA to s 102BF.

DISCUSSION

The law in relation to lack of forensic purpose is found in cases including National Employers’ Mutual General Association Ltd v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372 and Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors (2000) FLC 93-038; [2000] FamCA 892. The parents and the Independent Children’s Lawyer agreed that the Court should first inspect the documents for itself to rule on the objection. The Court did so (see s 102BD(5) of the Act). The only documents read, however, were those that fell within the parameters of the objection. Thus, the inspection was limited to documents produced in relation to the mother and within the timeframe of the subpoena. The documents identified above were inspected and were found to be routine records of the mother’s engagement with the professionals from medical and allied health services who the mother has consulted in relation to her health and arising from the present litigation; also, from the time the various orders were made and also the subsequent proceedings to date.

The Court was not able to identify a document that fell or was likely to fall within the provisions of s 102BE of the Act, and specifically, having regard to the Court’s long involvement in this matter and its extensive findings, it was not possible to conclude that harm as defined in s 102BE(2) was likely to or might be caused directly or indirectly to the mother or the children, but even if that were the case in the circumstances of this case, it is likely that the probative value of this evidence would predicate the dismissal of the objection.

DETERMINATION

The parties were ordered to do certain things and the clear and legitimate purpose of the subpoena was to produce relevant information specifically related to this. The objection to lack of forensic purpose, which, for present purposes, encapsulates the width of the subpoena, has no foundation. The documents only seek information from 18 March 2025. It is proportionate to the remaining issues before the Court. The notice of objection is dismissed. Leave to inspect the documents is granted to the Independent Children’s Lawyer only, for the time being.

Notwithstanding the Court’s findings about the absence of a protected confidence, in circumstances where both parents are currently representing themselves it remains appropriate and in the best interests of the children that only the Independent Children’s Lawyer have leave to inspect. Indeed, the Independent Children’s Lawyer had foreshadowed to address the mother’s concerns, that no photocopy access would be sought and inspection take place in person only. The Court will accede to this but will remain open to varying this restriction in relation to inspection, especially, for example, if and when the parents get legal representation themselves.
html.en:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These brief oral reasons explain why a Notice of Objection – Subpoena (“notice of objection”) has been dismissed. The subpoena was filed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer on 27 March 2026 and is directed to the Proper Officer, O Health District. The documents sought are as follows:

A copy of all documents, including but not limited to, clinical notes, medical notes, attendance records, assessments, admissions, treatment diagnosis/prognosis, discharge summaries, medical certificates, referrals, correspondence and all other writings from 18 March 2025 to date in relation to the parents and two children.

BACKGROUND

The notice of objection was filed on 9 April 2026 by the Respondent Mother (“the mother”). There are eight stated grounds. There is no need to repeat them here. They fall into two broad categories:

(1) an objection based on protected confidences under s 102BA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”); and

(2) an objection based on lack of legitimate forensic purpose.

The substantive proceedings are part-heard. They are complex proceedings relating to two children. On 19 November 2025, I published reasons for judgment explaining both the final and the interim orders that I made in relation to the children (see Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834). An appeal has been lodged in relation to the orders, which has been heard, but not yet determined. For present purposes, the orders relevantly provide for both the mother, the father and the children to do certain things, including participating in therapy. The case is part-heard until later this year. An objection based on a protected confidence is based on Division 1B of Part XI of the Act, being s 102BA to s 102BF.

DISCUSSION

The law in relation to lack of forensic purpose is found in cases including National Employers’ Mutual General Association Ltd v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372 and Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors (2000) FLC 93-038; [2000] FamCA 892. The parents and the Independent Children’s Lawyer agreed that the Court should first inspect the documents for itself to rule on the objection. The Court did so (see s 102BD(5) of the Act). The only documents read, however, were those that fell within the parameters of the objection. Thus, the inspection was limited to documents produced in relation to the mother and within the timeframe of the subpoena. The documents identified above were inspected and were found to be routine records of the mother’s engagement with the professionals from medical and allied health services who the mother has consulted in relation to her health and arising from the present litigation; also, from the time the various orders were made and also the subsequent proceedings to date.

The Court was not able to identify a document that fell or was likely to fall within the provisions of s 102BE of the Act, and specifically, having regard to the Court’s long involvement in this matter and its extensive findings, it was not possible to conclude that harm as defined in s 102BE(2) was likely to or might be caused directly or indirectly to the mother or the children, but even if that were the case in the circumstances of this case, it is likely that the probative value of this evidence would predicate the dismissal of the objection.

DETERMINATION

The parties were ordered to do certain things and the clear and legitimate purpose of the subpoena was to produce relevant information specifically related to this. The objection to lack of forensic purpose, which, for present purposes, encapsulates the width of the subpoena, has no foundation. The documents only seek information from 18 March 2025. It is proportionate to the remaining issues before the Court. The notice of objection is dismissed. Leave to inspect the documents is granted to the Independent Children’s Lawyer only, for the time being.

Notwithstanding the Court’s findings about the absence of a protected confidence, in circumstances where both parents are currently representing themselves it remains appropriate and in the best interests of the children that only the Independent Children’s Lawyer have leave to inspect. Indeed, the Independent Children’s Lawyer had foreshadowed to address the mother’s concerns, that no photocopy access would be sought and inspection take place in person only. The Court will accede to this but will remain open to varying this restriction in relation to inspection, especially, for example, if and when the parents get legal representation themselves.

sc.zh: 受保护私密信息的定义

desc.zh: 界定何为「受保护私密信息」——本案中母亲据此对其健康记录提出异议。
desc.en: Defines a 'protected confidence' — the basis on which the mother objected to her health records here.

sc.zh: 处理私密信息异议的程序

desc.zh: 规定法院处理受保护私密信息异议的程序;第 (5) 款允许法院先自行查阅相关文件再作裁定。
desc.en: Sets out how the court deals with protected-confidence objections; sub (5) lets the court inspect the documents itself before ruling.

sc.zh: 损害与证明价值的权衡

desc.zh: 要求法院考量披露是否可能造成「损害」（第 (2) 款界定），并与证据的证明价值相权衡。
desc.en: Requires the court to consider whether disclosure might cause 'harm' (defined in sub (2)) and weigh it against the probative value of the evidence.

sc.zh: 私密信息分部的配套条款

desc.zh: 第 1B 分部（受保护私密信息）中与上述条文配套的条款。
desc.en: A provision within Division 1B (protected confidences) operating alongside the sections above.

t.zh: National Employers' Mutual General Association Ltd v Waind and Hill
t.en: National Employers' Mutual General Association Ltd v Waind and Hill

court.zh: 新南威尔士州上诉法院 · 1978

summary.zh: 传票须有正当取证目的，所调取文件须与争点相关。
summary.en: A subpoena must have a legitimate forensic purpose and seek documents relevant to the issues.

prop.zh: 无正当取证目的或不相关的传票应被撤销。
prop.en: A subpoena lacking forensic purpose or relevance should be set aside.

t.zh: Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors
t.en: Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors

summary.zh: 在家事语境下适用正当取证目的原则审查传票。
summary.en: Applies the legitimate-forensic-purpose principle to subpoenas in the family law context.

prop.zh: 家事传票同样须经正当取证目的检验。
prop.en: Family law subpoenas are equally subject to the forensic-purpose test.

summary.zh: 同一双方的实体判决，含子女终局与临时命令及法院的大量认定。
summary.en: The substantive judgment between the same parties, with final and interim orders for the children and extensive findings.

prop.zh: 提供本程序的实体背景与命令依据。
prop.en: Provides the substantive background and the basis of the orders in this matter.

fulltext:
ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

These brief oral reasons explain why a Notice of Objection – Subpoena (“notice of objection”) has been dismissed. The subpoena was filed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer on 27 March 2026 and is directed to the Proper Officer, O Health District. The documents sought are as follows:
A copy of all documents, including but not limited to, clinical notes, medical notes, attendance records, assessments, admissions, treatment diagnosis/prognosis, discharge summaries, medical certificates, referrals, correspondence and all other writings from 18 March 2025 to date in relation to the parents and two children.

BACKGROUND

The notice of objection was filed on 9 April 2026 by the Respondent Mother (“the mother”). There are eight stated grounds. There is no need to repeat them here. They fall into two broad categories:
(1) an objection based on protected confidences under s 102BA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”); and

(2) an objection based on lack of legitimate forensic purpose.

The substantive proceedings are part-heard. They are complex proceedings relating to two children. On 19 November 2025, I published reasons for judgment explaining both the final and the interim orders that I made in relation to the children (see Stephanidi & Lawrence (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 834). An appeal has been lodged in relation to the orders, which has been heard, but not yet determined. For present purposes, the orders relevantly provide for both the mother, the father and the children to do certain things, including participating in therapy. The case is part-heard until later this year. An objection based on a protected confidence is based on Division 1B of Part XI of the Act, being s 102BA to s 102BF.

DISCUSSION

The law in relation to lack of forensic purpose is found in cases including National Employers’ Mutual General Association Ltd v Waind and Hill [1978] 1 NSWLR 372 and Hatton v Attorney-General of Commonwealth of Australia & Ors (2000) FLC 93-038; [2000] FamCA 892. The parents and the Independent Children’s Lawyer agreed that the Court should first inspect the documents for itself to rule on the objection. The Court did so (see s 102BD(5) of the Act). The only documents read, however, were those that fell within the parameters of the objection. Thus, the inspection was limited to documents produced in relation to the mother and within the timeframe of the subpoena. The documents identified above were inspected and were found to be routine records of the mother’s engagement with the professionals from medical and allied health services who the mother has consulted in relation to her health and arising from the present litigation; also, from the time the various orders were made and also the subsequent proceedings to date.
The Court was not able to identify a document that fell or was likely to fall within the provisions of s 102BE of the Act, and specifically, having regard to the Court’s long involvement in this matter and its extensive findings, it was not possible to conclude that harm as defined in s 102BE(2) was likely to or might be caused directly or indirectly to the mother or the children, but even if that were the case in the circumstances of this case, it is likely that the probative value of this evidence would predicate the dismissal of the objection.

DETERMINATION

The parties were ordered to do certain things and the clear and legitimate purpose of the subpoena was to produce relevant information specifically related to this. The objection to lack of forensic purpose, which, for present purposes, encapsulates the width of the subpoena, has no foundation. The documents only seek information from 18 March 2025. It is proportionate to the remaining issues before the Court. The notice of objection is dismissed. Leave to inspect the documents is granted to the Independent Children’s Lawyer only, for the time being.
Notwithstanding the Court’s findings about the absence of a protected confidence, in circumstances where both parents are currently representing themselves it remains appropriate and in the best interests of the children that only the Independent Children’s Lawyer have leave to inspect. Indeed, the Independent Children’s Lawyer had foreshadowed to address the mother’s concerns, that no photocopy access would be sought and inspection take place in person only. The Court will accede to this but will remain open to varying this restriction in relation to inspection, especially, for example, if and when the parents get legal representation themselves.
